news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in understanding Earth's ancient geology, increased support for young scientists in China, and innovative bioengineering enabling cyborg cockroaches to breathe underwater.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans la compréhension de la géologie ancienne de la Terre, un soutien accru aux jeunes scientifiques en Chine et une bio-ingénierie innovante permettant à des cafards cyborgs de respirer sous l'eau.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en la comprensión de la geología antigua de la Tierra, el mayor apoyo a los jóvenes científicos en China y una innovadora técnica de bioingeniería que permite a las cucarachas cyborg respirar bajo el agua.

articles.title: A volcano has erupted remnants of Earth's primordial magma ocean
articles.summary: Earth was once covered by a global magma ocean, which later cooled and crystallised – now traces of this primordial event have been found in magma from a young volcano in the Indian Ocean
articles.content:
Earth was once covered by a global magma ocean, which later cooled and crystallised – now traces of this primordial event have been found in magma from a young volcano in the Indian Ocean

Submarine reliefs produced by a survey off the coast of Mayotte in 2019, showing the new underwater volcano Fani Maoré Campagne MAYOBS2

A rising underwater volcano off the coast of Madagascar has been spewing up chemical traces of material from a primordial magma ocean in the first 100 million years of Earth’s history.

Scientists generally suspect that Earth’s mantle – the thick layer of hot rock beneath the crust – has been steadily churning for more than 4 billion years, gradually erasing most chemical traces of the planet’s earliest history.

“This is going to change a lot of things [in earth science], because now we have proof that materials dating back 4.5 billion years – from the very beginning of Earth’s history – still exist in sufficient quantities to be sampled in a volcano,” says Catherine Chauvel at the French National Centre for Scientific Research (CNRS) in Paris.

During the Hadean eon, a Mars-sized object collided with Earth, breaking off debris that scientists believe later formed the moon. The impact heated the young planet so intensely that it became covered by a global magma ocean. Over the next few million years, the molten rock cooled and crystallised, and the earliest crust began to form above the mantle.

Some scientists suspected that traces of this primordial crystallisation survived in Earth’s mantle, but they lacked the analytical precision to prove it, says Chauvel.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

In May 2018, an unusual swarm of earthquakes off the French island of Mayotte, between Madagascar and Mozambique, led scientists to discover a new volcano about 50 kilometres further east, named Fani Maoré. Eruptions over the following three years drained so much magma from underneath Mayotte that it sank about 20 centimetres.

Chauvel and her colleagues recovered volcanic rock samples from Fani Maoré and nearby Mayotte to compare the chemistry of the new volcano with that of the older volcanic system. They teamed up with Claudine Israel at the University of Cambridge to investigate further, using a newly developed ultra-precise technique to measure tiny differences in neodymium isotopes. Such isotopes preserve a chemical record of how Earth’s primordial magma ocean crystallised as the young planet cooled, says Israel.

The team found that, compared to Mayotte’s lava, the Fani Maoré lava had a slightly higher ratio of neodymium-142 to neodymium-144. That higher ratio probably reflects a pocket of ancient mantle that escaped billions of years of mixing and is still relatively rich in bridgmanite – a mineral thought to have been among the first to crystallise from Earth’s primordial magma ocean.

“It’s always exciting to find something you’ve been looking for – and that nobody else has found yet,” says Chauvel.

The findings suggest Earth’s mantle might never have become as thoroughly mixed as many geologists had assumed. That could help scientists reconstruct how Earth’s primordial magma ocean solidified, says Israel.

“For the first time, we’ve shown experimentally how the mantle crystallised from the magma ocean, and how that crystallisation created chemical heterogeneity from the very beginning,” she says.

The new findings provide plausible evidence that Earth’s mantle still preserves extremely ancient material, says Tim Johnson at Curtin University in Perth, Australia. “This seems to be an exciting advance,” he says.

“It takes an enormous amount of work to get a technique like that working properly, and it looks like they’ve succeeded,” says Bernard Bourdon at the CNRS in Lyon.

The study offers an unprecedented glimpse into a period of Earth’s history for which almost no direct evidence survives, he adds. “It’s a bit like discovering a sample of Earth’s core that somehow made it all the way to the surface,” says Bourdon.

For Richard Carlson at Carnegie Science in Washington, D.C., the precision alone is noteworthy. “Anyone who has experience with these measurements would recognise it as a major achievement,” he says.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in geoscience: the discovery of primordial magma ocean remnants in a young underwater volcano. This finding provides new insights into Earth's early history and mantle composition, representing a major advancement in understanding planetary formation and geology with broad scientific and educational impact.
articles.personality_title: Underwater volcano reveals clues from Earth’s earliest magma ocean
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Long ago, Earth was covered by a global ocean of molten rock called a magma ocean. Over time, this hot liquid cooled and formed the solid mantle and crust beneath the surface. Scientists have wondered if any material from that ancient magma ocean still exists today.

**What happened** – Recently, a team of scientists studied lava from a young underwater volcano called Fani Maoré, located near the island of Mayotte in the Indian Ocean. This volcano began erupting after earthquakes were detected in 2018. Using a new, highly precise method, researchers analyzed tiny differences in neodymium isotopes in the lava. These chemical clues showed the lava contains material from Earth’s original magma ocean, dating back about 4.5 billion years.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it proves that parts of Earth’s earliest mantle have survived billions of years without mixing completely. It challenges previous ideas that Earth’s mantle is fully mixed and uniform. Finding this ancient material helps scientists better understand how the planet’s first solid layers formed and how Earth’s deep interior has changed over time.

**What’s next step** – Scientists plan to use this new method to study other volcanoes and mantle samples around the world. This could reveal more about Earth’s early history and the processes that shaped our planet’s interior. It may also improve models of how planets form and evolve.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Lava from a young underwater volcano has preserved chemical traces from Earth’s first magma ocean, offering a rare glimpse into the planet’s formation over 4 billion years ago.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un volcan sous-marin révèle des indices de l’océan de magma primordial de la Terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a très longtemps, la Terre était recouverte d’un océan mondial de roche en fusion appelé océan de magma. Avec le temps, ce liquide chaud a refroidi et formé le manteau et la croûte solides sous la surface. Les scientifiques se demandaient si une partie de ce matériau ancien existait encore aujourd’hui.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Récemment, une équipe de chercheurs a étudié la lave d’un jeune volcan sous-marin nommé Fani Maoré, situé près de l’île de Mayotte dans l’océan Indien. Ce volcan a commencé à entrer en éruption après des séismes détectés en 2018. Grâce à une nouvelle méthode très précise, les chercheurs ont analysé de petites différences dans les isotopes de néodyme dans la lave. Ces indices chimiques montrent que la lave contient du matériau provenant de l’océan de magma originel de la Terre, vieux d’environ 4,5 milliards d’années.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle prouve que certaines parties du manteau terrestre le plus ancien ont survécu pendant des milliards d’années sans se mélanger complètement. Elle remet en cause l’idée que le manteau de la Terre est entièrement homogène. Trouver ce matériau ancien aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre comment se sont formées les premières couches solides de la planète et comment l’intérieur de la Terre a évolué.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques prévoient d’utiliser cette nouvelle méthode pour étudier d’autres volcans et échantillons du manteau à travers le monde. Cela pourrait révéler plus d’informations sur l’histoire ancienne de la Terre et les processus qui ont façonné son intérieur. Cela pourrait aussi améliorer les modèles de formation et d’évolution des planètes.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La lave d’un jeune volcan sous-marin a conservé des traces chimiques de l’océan de magma originel de la Terre, offrant un rare aperçu de la formation de la planète il y a plus de 4 milliards d’années.
articles.personality_title_es: Un volcán submarino revela pistas del océano de magma primordial de la Tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace mucho tiempo, la Tierra estuvo cubierta por un océano global de roca fundida llamado océano de magma. Con el tiempo, este líquido caliente se enfrió y formó el manto y la corteza sólidos bajo la superficie. Los científicos se preguntaban si algún material de ese antiguo océano de magma aún existía hoy.

**Qué pasó** – Recientemente, un equipo de científicos estudió lava de un volcán submarino joven llamado Fani Maoré, ubicado cerca de la isla de Mayotte en el Océano Índico. Este volcán comenzó a entrar en erupción tras una serie de terremotos detectados en 2018. Usando un método nuevo y muy preciso, los investigadores analizaron pequeñas diferencias en isótopos de neodimio en la lava. Estas pistas químicas mostraron que la lava contiene material del océano de magma original de la Tierra, con unos 4.5 mil millones de años.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque demuestra que partes del manto más antiguo de la Tierra han sobrevivido miles de millones de años sin mezclarse completamente. Cuestiona la idea previa de que el manto terrestre está completamente mezclado y es uniforme. Encontrar este material antiguo ayuda a los científicos a entender mejor cómo se formaron las primeras capas sólidas del planeta y cómo ha cambiado el interior de la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos planean usar este nuevo método para estudiar otros volcanes y muestras del manto en todo el mundo. Esto podría revelar más sobre la historia temprana de la Tierra y los procesos que moldearon su interior. También podría mejorar los modelos de formación y evolución planetaria.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La lava de un joven volcán submarino ha conservado trazas químicas del primer océano de magma de la Tierra, ofreciendo una rara mirada a la formación del planeta hace más de 4 mil millones de años.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing underwater volcano gently erupting ancient molten magma, with layers beneath the ocean surface symbolizing Earth's primordial magma ocean crystallizing into early mantle rock, all rendered in soft natural hues of deep blues, fiery oranges, and earthy browns.

articles.title: China boosts prestigious grants for young scientists — will it ease competition?
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 03 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01989-5The National Natural Science Foundation of China says it will fund an extra 12,000 projects from this year.
articles.content:
Early-career scientists in China face intense competition for entry-level jobs. Credit: Xinhua/Shutterstock

China’s main research funding agency will increase its number of prestigious grants for early-career scientists by 50%. The move is part of the country’s promise to step up support for young scientists, who face intense competition to secure funding and jobs. Early-career researchers have welcomed the news, but some academics say it won’t be a silver bullet.

The National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC), the country’s main funder of fundamental research, intends to finance an extra 12,000 projects this year through its Young Scientists Fund, equivalent to a budget boost of around 3.6 billion yuan (US$531 million). Projects are typically proposed by individuals, rather than groups.

“I was thrilled when I heard the news because more of us will be able to get grants,” says Shi, a 27-year-old postdoctoral student of electronic science and technology in Hefei, China, who has applied for a grant this year. He asked to share only by his surname to protect his identity.

In a statement published in May, the NSFC said that it would provide the extra sponsorships through one of the fund’s grant programmes, called C category, a highly competitive programme that is widely regarded as an entry ticket to the country’s research system. Last year, the NSFC handed out a total of 7.2 billion yuan to 24,051 selected projects through this category.

Can China keep up its extraordinary research growth?

“In many Chinese universities, early-career academics must win a grant under the NSFC’s Young Scientists Fund to find a job, get a promotion or secure tenure,” says Tong Xinzhao, a bioinformatician at the Xi’an Jiaotong–Liverpool University in Suzhou, China. Tong won a C-category grant last year after missing out four times.

Most early-career researchers apply for the C category because it is considered to be the most realistic route to obtain funding, Tong says.

C-category grants are open to postdoctoral candidates, junior researchers and some graduate students. Applicants must be under 35 years old if they are male, or 40 years old if they are female.

The agency is expected to announce who secured this year’s grants in August.

Boosting basic research

The fact that the Chinese government intends to expand the Young Scientists Fund by such a big margin is notable, says Li Jiang, an information scientist at Nanjing University in China, who has studied the challenges that young Chinese researchers face.

Competition for all funds managed by the NSFC has been fierce over the past few years owing to the surging number of applicants — despite the government continuously increasing the agency’s budget. For the C category, which was simply known as the Young Scientists Fund before 2025, its funding more than doubled between 2015 and 2025, but the success rate for applicants being awarded funding dropped from 24.58% to 14.38%, according to official numbers.

The boost to the Young Scientists Fund directly addresses the relentless competition these young people face by providing them with real and substantial resources, Jiang Li adds.

The news also sends a clear signal that the government considers young researchers as central to its plan of producing more discoveries in foundational sciences, says Li Jizhen, an innovation policy researcher at Tsinghua University in Beijing. In April, China’s President Xi Jinping directed the country to strengthen basic research.

Fierce competition

Many young researchers say that a big problem they face is their ‘up or out’ contracts at universities; these typically last for six years, after which researchers need to obtain tenure or otherwise face dismissal. This, plus the age restrictions for the Young Scientists Fund, makes the competition for grants particularly brutal.

“If you think about how old a student usually is when they finish a PhD programme, they don’t have many years left to try for this grant in order to set up a career,” says Tong. Some candidates need to balance grant applications with their plans to have children, she adds.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant increase in funding for early-career scientists in China, which addresses intense competition and supports foundational scientific research. This funding boost has broad implications for the scientific community and innovation, benefiting society through potential discoveries and technological advancement.
articles.personality_title: China expands grants to support young scientists amid fierce competition
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In China, young scientists often struggle to get funding and jobs because many people compete for limited opportunities. The National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) is the main agency that gives money to support scientific research.

**What happened** – This year, the NSFC decided to increase the number of grants for early-career scientists by 12,000 projects, which is 50% more than before. These grants come from the Young Scientists Fund, especially the C category program, which is important for young researchers to start their careers. The funding boost is about 3.6 billion yuan (US$531 million).

**Impact** – This increase helps young scientists who often need these grants to get jobs, promotions, or tenure at universities. Because the competition has been very tough, more grants mean more chances for new researchers to continue their work and make discoveries. It also shows that the Chinese government wants to focus on basic science research and support young talent.

**What's next step** – The NSFC will announce the winners of the new grants in August. This extra support might encourage more young people to stay in science and help China keep growing as a leader in research.

**One-sentence takeaway** – China’s large funding increase for young scientists aims to ease tough competition and boost foundational research by giving more early-career researchers a chance to succeed.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Chine augmente les subventions pour soutenir les jeunes scientifiques face à une forte concurrence
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – En Chine, les jeunes scientifiques ont souvent du mal à obtenir des financements et des emplois à cause d’une forte concurrence. La Fondation nationale des sciences naturelles de Chine (NSFC) est l’agence principale qui finance la recherche scientifique.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Cette année, la NSFC a décidé d’augmenter de 12 000 le nombre de subventions pour les jeunes chercheurs, soit une hausse de 50 %. Ces subventions proviennent du Fonds pour les jeunes scientifiques, notamment du programme catégorie C, important pour lancer la carrière des jeunes chercheurs. Cette augmentation représente environ 3,6 milliards de yuans (531 millions de dollars).

**Impact** – Cette augmentation aide les jeunes scientifiques qui ont souvent besoin de ces subventions pour obtenir un emploi, une promotion ou une titularisation à l’université. La concurrence étant très forte, plus de subventions signifie plus de chances pour ces chercheurs de poursuivre leurs travaux et de faire des découvertes. Cela montre aussi que le gouvernement chinois veut renforcer la recherche fondamentale et soutenir les jeunes talents.

**Prochaine étape** – La NSFC annoncera en août les bénéficiaires des nouvelles subventions. Ce soutien supplémentaire pourrait encourager davantage de jeunes à rester dans la science et aider la Chine à continuer de progresser dans la recherche.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La forte augmentation des financements pour les jeunes scientifiques en Chine vise à réduire la concurrence difficile et à renforcer la recherche fondamentale en offrant à plus de jeunes chercheurs la possibilité de réussir.
articles.personality_title_es: China amplía las becas para apoyar a jóvenes científicos en medio de fuerte competencia
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En China, los jóvenes científicos a menudo enfrentan dificultades para obtener financiamiento y empleos debido a la alta competencia. La Fundación Nacional de Ciencias Naturales de China (NSFC) es la principal agencia que financia la investigación científica.

**Qué pasó** – Este año, la NSFC decidió aumentar en 12,000 el número de becas para científicos en etapa temprana, un aumento del 50 %. Estas becas provienen del Fondo para Jóvenes Científicos, especialmente del programa categoría C, que es clave para que los jóvenes investigadores comiencen su carrera. El aumento representa unos 3.6 mil millones de yuanes (531 millones de dólares).

**Impacto** – Este aumento ayuda a los jóvenes científicos que suelen necesitar estas becas para conseguir empleo, ascensos o estabilidad en las universidades. Debido a la fuerte competencia, más becas significan más oportunidades para que continúen sus trabajos y hagan descubrimientos. También muestra que el gobierno chino quiere enfocarse en la investigación básica y apoyar a los jóvenes talentos.

**Próximo paso** – La NSFC anunciará en agosto a los ganadores de las nuevas becas. Este apoyo extra podría motivar a más jóvenes a seguir en la ciencia y ayudar a China a mantener su crecimiento en investigación.

**Frase clave** – El gran aumento de fondos para jóvenes científicos en China busca aliviar la dura competencia y fortalecer la investigación básica al dar más oportunidades a investigadores en etapa temprana.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a young sapling flourishing amid a dense forest of slender bamboo stalks, each bamboo representing early-career scientists reaching upward, with golden light gently illuminating the sapling to symbolize new prestigious grants nurturing growth and hope in a competitive environment.

articles.title: Daily briefing: ‘Cyborg’ cockroaches breathe underwater with printed suit
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 02 July 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02108-0The insects could explore underwater for up to three hours. Plus, fake cancer studies rake in the citations and how scientists can build trust by recognizing their biases.
articles.content:
The insects could explore underwater for up to three hours. Plus, fake cancer studies rake in the citations and how scientists can build trust by recognizing their biases.

Hello Nature readers, would you like to get this Briefing in your inbox free every day? Sign up here.

Remote-controlled insects could be used in search and rescue missions to explore dangerous areas or rubble that humans can’t access, researchers hope. (NTU Singapore)

Researchers have equipped ‘cyborg’ cockroaches with diving suits that enable the insects to breathe underwater. These Madagascar hissing cockroaches (Gromphadorhina portentosa) have electrodes attached to their brains and sensory organs that allow researchers to remotely control their movements. The new 3D-printed suits attach tubes to the insects’ breathing holes, called spiracles, that connect to a chemical oxygen generator. In underwater tests, the roaches could explore a variety of underwater terrain and traverse obstacles for up to three hours at speeds similar to those they could move at on land.

Popular Science | 6 min read

Reference: Nature Communications paper

Researchers have created an entirely synthetic cell that can feed, grow and reproduce. Dubbed ‘SpudCells’, the tiny blobs aren’t technically alive, but can perform some of the chemical reactions seen in living cells. “It’s a cell that was built, not born. It’s constructed, but it does what cells do,” says synthetic biologist Drew Endy. Scientists hope that humanmade cells like these can reveal secrets such as how many genes a cell needs to survive, or be engineered to produce compounds that natural cells can’t make.

The New York Times | 8 min read

Reference: biotic preprint (not peer reviewed)

Cancer research papers that appear to have been produced by paper mills — businesses that produce and sell low-quality or fraudulent manuscripts — garner double the citations than do genuine articles in the field. The problem appears to be self-propagating: in an analysis of tens of thousands of papers, researchers found that papers that were probably produced by paper mills frequently cite, or are cited by, other potentially fraudulent articles. This distortion of citations might also inflate the impact metrics of journals in molecular oncology, says statistician and study co-author Adrian Barnett.

Nature | 6 min read

Reference: bioRxiv preprint (not peer reviewed)

Features & opinion

Scientists need to acknowledge their own political biases to avoid eroding public trust in science, argues Mark Henderson, director of corporate affairs at the charitable foundation Wellcome. The scientific community can’t assume public support for their positions and should take care to ensure that science isn’t equated with membership in a wider liberal or progressive project. “If respect for science is bundled with wider political identity, people with more conservative values might feel pushed to reject science along with the politics,” Henderson writes.

Nature | 6 min read

In the waters of the Port of Miami, Florida, a community of coral is thriving — seemingly against all odds. Growing on hard materials such as concrete blocks and dumped shopping trolleys, the coral have stood strong in the face of disease, drastic swings in temperature and pollution that have caused bleaching in other Florida coral populations. The coral in the busy waters of the port are only a few decades old, and scientists think that human activity in the area could be what’s equipping these species with their unusual survival mechanisms. Now researchers are trying to work out how.

bioGraphic | 9 min read

In The Political Economy of Rwanda’s Rise, economist Pritish Behuria details Rwanda’s bold attempt to transform its economy after the 1994 genocide. The nation has made impressive gains in health systems, technology and governance, but that has yet to translate into an industrial economy, Behuria writes. The book “is about much more than Rwanda”, says science writer Abdullahi Tsanni in his review. It “offers an important lesson: growth is not the same as transformation, and technological capability remains extremely difficult for nations to build”.

Nature | 7 min read
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a significant technological innovation where researchers have developed 'cyborg' cockroaches equipped with 3D-printed diving suits that allow them to breathe underwater for up to three hours. This breakthrough has broad potential applications in search and rescue missions in hazardous environments inaccessible to humans, representing a meaningful real-world impact. The article provides sufficient detail about the technology, its function, and its potential uses, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create cockroaches that can breathe underwater for rescue missions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Researchers are looking for new ways to explore dangerous places where humans cannot safely go. Insects like cockroaches can move through tight spaces, making them useful for search and rescue work.

**What happened** – Scientists developed special diving suits for Madagascar hissing cockroaches. These suits are 3D-printed and connect to the cockroaches’ breathing holes, supplying oxygen from a chemical generator. The cockroaches also have tiny electrodes attached to their brains and sensors, allowing researchers to control their movements remotely. In tests, the cockroaches could swim underwater for up to three hours while moving as fast as they do on land.

**Impact** – This technology is important because it lets small, remote-controlled insects explore underwater areas that might be too dangerous or hard for people or machines to reach. For example, they could help find survivors trapped in flooded rubble after a disaster. Using living insects combined with technology creates new possibilities for rescue work.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely continue improving the suits and control systems to make the cockroaches more reliable and able to explore different environments. They may also test the insects in real-life search and rescue situations to see how well they perform.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have made cockroaches that can breathe underwater and be controlled remotely, opening new ways to explore dangerous places during emergencies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent des cafards capables de respirer sous l'eau pour les missions de secours
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les chercheurs cherchent de nouvelles façons d’explorer des endroits dangereux où les humains ne peuvent pas aller en toute sécurité. Les insectes comme les cafards peuvent se faufiler dans des espaces étroits, ce qui les rend utiles pour les missions de recherche et de secours.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé des combinaisons de plongée spéciales pour les cafards bourdonnants de Madagascar. Ces combinaisons, imprimées en 3D, se connectent aux trous respiratoires des cafards et leur fournissent de l’oxygène grâce à un générateur chimique. Les cafards ont aussi de petits électrodes attachées à leur cerveau et à leurs capteurs, permettant aux chercheurs de contrôler leurs mouvements à distance. Lors des tests, les cafards ont pu nager sous l’eau jusqu’à trois heures tout en se déplaçant aussi vite que sur terre.

**Impact** – Cette technologie est importante car elle permet à de petits insectes télécommandés d’explorer des zones sous-marines trop dangereuses ou difficiles d’accès pour les humains ou les machines. Par exemple, ils pourraient aider à retrouver des survivants piégés dans des décombres inondés après une catastrophe. Utiliser des insectes vivants combinés à la technologie crée de nouvelles possibilités pour les secours.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement continuer à améliorer les combinaisons et les systèmes de contrôle pour rendre les cafards plus fiables et capables d’explorer différents environnements. Ils pourraient aussi tester les insectes dans des situations réelles de recherche et de secours pour vérifier leur efficacité.

**Phrase clé** – Des scientifiques ont fabriqué des cafards capables de respirer sous l’eau et d’être contrôlés à distance, ouvrant de nouvelles façons d’explorer des endroits dangereux en cas d’urgence.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean cucarachas que pueden respirar bajo el agua para misiones de rescate
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los investigadores buscan nuevas formas de explorar lugares peligrosos donde los humanos no pueden entrar con seguridad. Insectos como las cucarachas pueden moverse por espacios estrechos, lo que los hace útiles para misiones de búsqueda y rescate.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron trajes de buceo especiales para cucarachas sonoras de Madagascar. Estos trajes están impresos en 3D y se conectan a los orificios respiratorios de las cucarachas, suministrándoles oxígeno mediante un generador químico. Las cucarachas también tienen pequeños electrodos en sus cerebros y sensores que permiten a los investigadores controlar sus movimientos de forma remota. En pruebas, las cucarachas pudieron nadar bajo el agua hasta tres horas mientras se movían tan rápido como en tierra.

**Impacto** – Esta tecnología es importante porque permite que insectos pequeños y controlados a distancia exploren áreas submarinas que pueden ser demasiado peligrosas o difíciles para personas o máquinas. Por ejemplo, podrían ayudar a encontrar sobrevivientes atrapados en escombros inundados después de un desastre. Usar insectos vivos junto con tecnología crea nuevas posibilidades para el rescate.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente seguirán mejorando los trajes y los sistemas de control para hacer que las cucarachas sean más confiables y puedan explorar diferentes entornos. También podrían probar los insectos en situaciones reales de búsqueda y rescate para ver qué tan bien funcionan.

**Frase clave** – Científicos han creado cucarachas que pueden respirar bajo el agua y ser controladas remotamente, abriendo nuevas formas de explorar lugares peligrosos durante emergencias.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a Madagascar hissing cockroach wearing a delicate, translucent 3D-printed diving suit with tiny tubes connected to its spiracles, gently exploring an underwater scene filled with smooth rocks and soft aquatic plants, illuminated by soft natural light filtering through the water.
